coredata.dc:title: PL sensor for sensitive and selective detection of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol based on carbazole and tetraphenylsilane polymer
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Xue
Gao, Bo
Cui, Xu
Wang, Jihua
Dong, Wenyue
Duan, Qian
Fei, Teng
Su, Zhongmin
coredata.dc:description: A sensitive and selective detection of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP) is of great importance for the national security and pollution control. Herein, we report a novel 2,7-carbazole and tetraphenylsilane based polymer PCzSi, and applied it for nitroaromatics sensing. PCzSi shows a deep-blue photoluminescence (PL) emission peaking at 410 nm with a high glass transition temperature and good thermal stability. During the PL titration experiments, the polymer exhibited an obvious PL quenching phenomenon, with different extent towards different nitroaromatic analytes. Remarkably, a selective detection of TNP was realized using our polymer sensor, and the KSV value towards TNP is two orders of magnitude higher than 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene (TNT) and 1,3,5-trinitrobenzene (TNB). The sensing mechanism was further discussed to clarify the selectivity towards TNP based on the analysis of UV–vis absorption, excitation and PL spectra and cyclic voltammogram results. In addition, the paper strips were fabricated to detect both TNP solution and vapor, which demonstrates a good potential of our polymer for practical and on-site application as a solid PL sensor of TNP.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photoluminescence sensing
Explosive detection
Fluorescence
Nitroaromatics

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to thank the financial support from Science and Technology Development Plan Project of Jilin Provinc (grant number 20200201235JC ); the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant number 51803013 ); Jilin Association for Science and Technology (grant number 192011 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–vis absorption spectra and PL spectra of PCzSi in THF solution (10−5 M) and film state.
The cyclic voltammogram of PCzSi in acetonitrile (scanning speed: 50 mV/s, electrolyte: TBAPF6).
(a) Emission spectra of PCzSi solution with increasing amounts of TNP. (b) Stern-Volmer plots of PL intensity (I0/I–1) of PCzSi vs. TNP concentration.

Emission spectra of PCzSi solution with increasing amounts of (a) TNT, (b) TNB and (c) DNP. Stern-Volmer plots of PL intensity (I0/I–1) of PCzSi vs. (d) TNT, (e) TNB and (f) DNP concentration, respectively.
PL quenching degree of PCzSi upon individually adding various nitroaromatic analytes (phenol as comparison), followed by the addition of same amount of TNP.
(a) The UV–vis absorption spectra of TNP, TNT, TNB and DNP in THF solution. (b) The UV–vis absorption spectrum of TNP, and the excitation and PL spectra of PCzSi in THF solution.

Paper strips of PCzSi before and after dropping TNP solution (concentration: 0.01, 0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 1, 10, 25, 50, 100, and 1000 μg/mL, respectively), and after storage above TNP vapor for 25 min, under 365 nm excitation.
The synthetic routes to polymer PCzSi.
PL quenching data for TNP based on polymeric materials from the literature.

head.ce:title: PL sensor for sensitive and selective detection of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol based on carbazole and tetraphenylsilane polymer

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering
Changchun University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A sensitive and selective detection of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP) is of great importance for the national security and pollution control. Herein, we report a novel 2,7-carbazole and tetraphenylsilane based polymer PCzSi, and applied it for nitroaromatics sensing. PCzSi shows a deep-blue photoluminescence (PL) emission peaking at 410 nm with a high glass transition temperature and good thermal stability. During the PL titration experiments, the polymer exhibited an obvious PL quenching phenomenon, with different extent towards different nitroaromatic analytes. Remarkably, a selective detection of TNP was realized using our polymer sensor, and the KSV value towards TNP is two orders of magnitude higher than 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene (TNT) and 1,3,5-trinitrobenzene (TNB). The sensing mechanism was further discussed to clarify the selectivity towards TNP based on the analysis of UV–vis absorption, excitation and PL spectra and cyclic voltammogram results. In addition, the paper strips were fabricated to detect both TNP solution and vapor, which demonstrates a good potential of our polymer for practical and on-site application as a solid PL sensor of TNP.

ce:section.ce:para: 2,4,6-Trinitrophenol (TNP), also known as picric acid (PA), is a common high explosive, which was widely used during World War I. As an explosive, the explosive power of TNP is much superior than that of 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene (TNT), and is less stable, which is very threatening to the social safety and human lives if utilized by terrorists [1,2]. Besides, TNP has substantial applications in industry, such as dyes, medicine, insecticide, rubber, leather, and so on. The widespread use of TNP makes an inevitable leakage of it into our environment, as into groundwater and soil. It can be taken into human body via the skin, respiratory tract, digestive tract, etc., which is very toxic and hardly biodegraded. In consideration of its high toxicity and explosive capacity, to realize a sensitive and especially selective detection of TNP is of great importance. Till now, there are several detecting methods developed for TNP sensing, including ion mobility spectroscopy [3], gas chromatograph-mass spectroscopy [4], high performance liquid chromatography [5], electrochemistry analysis [6], X-ray diffraction [7], surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy [8], neutron analysis [9], and so on. However, there are some shortcomings existed, e.g. very expensive, complicated, or large instrument needed, which makes them not very suitable for the application of on-site TNP detection. Photoluminescence (PL) sensing is based on the interaction of PL material and the analyte, which is now researched for the sensing of a series of analytes, including non-bioanalytes such as nitroaromatic explosives [10–14], metal ions [15–17], and bioanalytes such as progesterone [18]. PL sensing possesses advantages of low-cost, easy operation, fast, and capability of on-site detection. The mechanism of PL sensing towards nitroaromatics is usually based on one or combination of photo-induced electron transfer and resonance energy transfer, which provides a probability of realizing selective detection of different nitroaromatics [19,20].

The reported PL sensing materials mainly include quantum dots (QDs) [21,22], metal organic frameworks (MOFs) [23,24], small molecules [25–27] and conjugated polymers (CPs) [28–30]. For example, Ni and Liu et al. reported two colored QD multilayer-decorated microchannel assays via a layer-by-layer assembly technique for rapid and sensitive detection of TNT with low detection limit [31]. Mandal et al. developed an amine-functionalized autofluorescent and polar 3D MOF {[Cd (ATAIA)]·4H2O}n for selective and ultrafast detection of TNP in water with a detection limit of 0.94 nM [32]. Hu and Zheng et al. reported a pH and thermal stable 3D framework [Mg (ATDC)(H2O)2]n with accessible –NH2 groups as a first highly selective Mg-MOF for sensing TNT [33]. Zhang et al. utilized 2-(perfluorophenyl)-1H-imidazo [4,5-b]phenazine (PFIPZ) containing imidazole and phenazine units for selective sensing TNP over other electron-deficient compounds or strong acids [34]. Park and Yoon et al. reported an excited-state intramolecular proton transfer (ESIPT)-based sensor (2-(benzothiazol-2-yl)pyren-1-ol) for sensitive detection of TNP [35]. Zhang et al. developed two triazole-based AIE materials, donor (D)-acceptor (A)-D type TPA-TAZ-TPA and D-A type TPA-TAZ-Ph, for 2,4-dinitrotoluene (DNT) vapor sensing with rapid response, high sensitivity and selectivity [36]. Till now, majority of reported PL sensing materials are based on conjugated polymers, since their ‘molecular wire’ effect based on energy migration first reported by Swager [37], which could effectively increase the sensitivity of PL chemosensors. For example, Scherf et al. reported an AIE type tetraphenylethylene-substituted polycarbazoles for amplified PL detection of TNB [12]. Iyer et al. synthesized a conjugated cationic polyfluorene (polyelectrolyte) derivative PFBT by means of simple and cost-effective oxidative coupling polymerization method, with remarkable fluorescence quenching response to TNP [29]. Patra et al. developed a series of π-conjugated A-alt-B type copolymers (P1–P3) appended with a 1,2,3-triazole moiety, exhibiting ppb-level detection of TNP [38]. Duan and Fei et al. developed water dispersible crosslinked fluorescent poly(triphenylamine-co-benzothiadiazole)s (PTPABT) nanoparticles via mini-emulsion polymerization for sensitive detection of 1,3,5-trinitrobenzene (TNB) [39]. Mothika et al. reported electrochemical generation of three conjugated microporous polymer thin films and their sensitive and selective fluorescence response to TNT in the vapor phase [40].

In this work, we have designed and synthesized a PL polymer based on carbazole and tetraphenylsilane moieties. Herein, the introduction of electron-rich carbazole unit could guarantee the strong interaction with electron-deficient nitroaromatic compounds, leading to a sensitive PL detection. Tetraphenylsilane unit was incorporated into the main chain in order to obtain a wide band gap polymer, whose excitation and/or PL spectra may overlap with the UV–visible (UV–vis) absorption of TNP, expecting an improved sensitivity towards TNP over other nitroaromatics via energy transfer mechanism, so the selective detection of TNP could be realized. In this manuscript, the chemical structure, thermal stability, electrochemical property and photophysical property of polymer PCzSi have been characterized in details. In addition, the PL quenching experiments towards nitroaromatics have been carried out, and as was expected, a highly sensitive and selective detection towards TNP has been realized based on the synthesized carbazole-tetraphenylsilane polymer PCzSi.
CAUTION! The nitroaromatic compounds used in PL sensing experiments are highly explosive and should be handled only in small amounts.

All reagents were obtained from commercial suppliers and were used without further purification. All reactions were carried out under nitrogen (N2) atmosphere by standard and Schlenk techniques. The solvents were used as commercial p.a. quality. 1H NMR, 13C NMR and 29Si NMR spectra were measured on AVANCZ 500 and Bruker AV 600 spectrometers at 298 K and tetramethylsilane as standard. UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on a UV-3100 spectrophotometer. PL and excitation spectra were carried out with a RF-5301PC fluorometer. Thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA) was undertaken on a PerkinElmer thermal analysis system at a heating rate of 10 °C/min and a nitrogen flow rate of 80 mL/min. Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) was performed on a NETZSCH (DSC-204) unit at a heating rate of 10 °C/min under nitrogen. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was performed by using a standard one-compartment, three-electrode electrochemical cell given by a BAS 100B/W electrochemical analyzer, with acetonitrile as solvent and 0.1 M tetrabutylammonium hexafluorophosphate (TBAPF6) as supporting electrolyte. The potentials were measured against an Ag/Ag+ reference electrode with ferrocene as the internal standard.

A solution of M1 (0.99 g, 2 mmol) in dry THF was cooled to −78 °C, then n-BuLi (2.5 M, 1.8 mL, 2.2 equiv.) was added dropwise to the solution. After stirred for 2 h, 2-isopropoxy-4,4,5,5-tetramethyl-1,3,2-dioxaborolane (0.45 mL, 2.2 mmol) was added dropwise in one portion. The mixture was warmed to room temperature and stirred for 48 h. Then the solution was poured to water and extracted with chloroform, and the organic phase was dried over anhydrous MgSO4. The crude product was purified by column chromatography to give the product as a white solid in 52% yield (0.61g). 1H NMR (CDCl3, 500 MHz, ppm): δ 7.80–7.79 (4H, d, J = 7.6 Hz, Ar–H), 7.58–7.53 (8H, m, Ar–H), 7.44–7.41 (2H, t, J = 7.3 Hz, Ar–H), 7.37–7.40 (4H, t, J = 7.3 Hz, Ar–H), 1.34 (24H, s, CH3). 13C NMR (CDCl3, 125 MHz, ppm): δ 138.03, 136.84, 136.12, 134.34, 130.08, 128.32, 84.27, 25.32. Elemental Anal. Calcd. for C36H42B2O4Si: C, 73.48%; H, 7.19%. Found: C, 73.50%; H, 7.33%.

Compound M2 (1051.5 mg, 0.26 mmol), 2,7-dibromo-9-hexyl-9H-carbazole (105.3 mg, 0.26 mmol), and Pd (dppf)Cl2 (6.4 mg, 0.0078 mmol) were dissolved in THF (4 mL) and 1 M aqueous potassium carbonate solution (1 mL). The mixture was degassed and then vigorously stirred at 75 °C for three days. At the end of the polymerization, phenylboronic acid (5 mol %) and bromobenzene (5 mol %) were added sequentially for end-capping process. The mixture was poured into water and extracted with dichloromethane then dried over anhydrous MgSO4. The solution was concentrated and purified by flash column chromatography to remove the catalyzer. After removal of the organic solvent, the condensed crude product was dropped into about 60 mL methanol to precipitate plenty of solid. The solid was collected by filtration and washed with methanol. Finally, the polymer was washed with acetone for 48 h in a Soxhlet apparatus and then dried under vacuum at 40 °C to afford the desired polymer as a light gray solid in 77% yield (116 mg). 1H NMR (CDCl3, 500 MHz, ppm): δ 8.16–8.15 (2H, d, J = 7.6 Hz, Ar–H), 7.79–7.75 (7H, m, Ar–H), 7.70–7.69 (5H, d, J = 6.7 Hz, Ar–H), 7.63 (3H, s, Ar–H), 7.53–7.52 (2H, d, J = 7.3 Hz, Ar–H), 7.48–7.40 (7H, m, Ar–H), 4.40–4.34 (2H, m, CH2), 1.93–1.91 (2H, m, CH2), 1.45–1.42 (2H, m, CH2), 1.32–1.27 (4H, m, CH2), 0.86–0.83 (3H, J = 7.0 Hz, CH3). 13C NMR (CDCl3, 125 MHz, ppm): δ 143.32, 141.56, 138.88, 136.96, 136.47, 134.32, 132.76, 129.71, 127.98, 127.08, 122.13, 120.71, 118.73, 107.29, 43.21, 31.62, 29.06, 27.07, 22.57, 14.01.29Si NMR (CDCl3, 119 MHz ppm): δ 0.01. Mn 9800, Mw 14,000, and Mw/Mn 1.43 (GPC, PS calibration).

Scheme 1 illustrates the synthetic routes to polymer PCzSi. Compound M1 (bis(4-bromophenyl)diphenylsilane) was synthesized using 1,4-dibromobenzene, n-BuLi (n-butyllithium) and diphenylsilane according to literature method [41]. The diboronic ester of tetraphenylsilane M2 was obtained by treatment of M1 with excess n-BuLi followed by addition of 2-isopropoxy-4,4,5,5-tetramethyl-1,3,2-dioxaborolane. Polymer PCzSi contains tetraphenylsilane connecting with 2,7-carbazole was generated in a palladium-catalyzed Suzuki-type coupling of monomer M2 and 2,7-dibromo-9-hexyl-9H-carbazole. The chemical structure of the obtained polymer was successfully confirmed by 1H NMR, 13C NMR DEPTQ (distorsionless enhancement by polarization transfer including the detection of the quaternary nuclei) and 29Si NMR spectra, as displayed in Fig. S1 and S2 in supplementary material. Furthermore, polymer PCzSi was finally characterized by GPC, TGA, DSC and optical spectroscopy.

The thermal properties of PCzSi were evaluated by DSC and TGA, shown in Fig. S3. After thermal annealing upon the first heating to 300 °C, only glass transition could be observed with glass transition temperature (Tg) of ~189 °C, and no crystallization and melting peak upon further heating beyond Tg. The onset of the thermal decomposition from TGA with 5% weight loss is ~438 °C for PCzSi, indicating its good thermal stability, which is important for the real application in solid state sensors.

The obtained polymer PCzSi is readily soluble in common organic solvents such as chloroform, toluene and THF but insoluble in ethanol and acetone. The UV–vis absorption and PL emission spectra of the polymer in dilute solution and film state are shown in Fig. 1 . PCzSi shows two absorption peaks at 269 and 335 nm in solution with the emission maximum at 410 nm. The PL quantum efficiency of PCzSi in THF solution was calculated to be 67% (using quinine sulfate as standard). The absorption spectrum of PCzSi in film is similar to that in solution with little red shift less than 3 nm, while the emission spectrum in thin-film is about 12 nm red-shifted compared with that in solution, and the full width at half-maximum in film increases by 7 nm compared with that in solution, which is caused by the aggregation of the polymer in the solid state. The optical band gap of PCzSi is 3.18 eV, estimated from their respective onset wavelengths of film absorption spectrum. The wide energy band gap is attributed to the presentence of silicon atoms in tetraphenylsilane unit, which could interrupt the conjugation extent of the polymer backbone.

The electrochemical properties of PCzSi were examined by CV, as shown in Fig. 2 . Polymer PCzSi shows an obvious oxidation process with the onset potential of 0.61 V and an irreversible reduction process occurred from −2.54 V. The reduction peak is attributed to the reduction of polymer backbone. The oxidation process is mainly contributed by the carbazole unit in the polymer, but the onset potential is slightly lower than that of solo carbazole, due to the conjugation between the phenyl ring of tetraphenylsilane and carbazole unit in the polymer backbone. The HOMO (highest-occupied molecular orbitals) and LUMO (lowest-unoccupied molecular orbitals) energy levels of PCzSi were calculated to be −5.33 eV and −2.20 eV, respectively, with following formulas: HOMO = - (EOx onset + 4.72) eV, LUMO = - (ERed onset + 4.72) eV. The onset potentials were determined from the intersection of two tangents drawn at the rising and background currents of the CV curve.

To test its potential as PL sensor for nitroaromatic explosives, a series of PL titration experiments were carried out using PCzSi solution (10−6 M in THF). Fig. 3 shows the PL spectra of PCzSi upon addition of different amounts of TNP and the Stern-Volmer plots. The PL of PCzSi was obviously quenched when adding TNP, and the PL intensity maximum at 410 nm weakened gradually with the addition of TNP. 90% of the PL intensity was quenched with TNP concentration of only 55 μM, and it is worthy to note that there is no change in the PL spectra during TNP addition, meaning that no other emissive species were formed during the quenching process. The Stern-Volmer plots indicate a high sensitivity of our polymer for PL quenching towards TNP, with a KSV of 1.4 × 105 M−1. In addition, another important parameter, limit of detection (LOD) [42], was calculated by the equation LOD = 3σ/m, where σ is the standard deviation estimated by the PL intensity of the blank PCzSi solution, and m represents the value of slope calculated by a linear fitting curve for the PL intensity vs. the concentration of TNP. It is found that PCzSi exhibits a low LOD value of 38 ppb (147 nM) towards TNP (Fig. S4,Table S1), thus demonstrating a sensitive detection of trace TNP using PCzSi sensor. For comparison, some literature data of KSV and LOD for the detection of TNP based on polymeric materials are summarized in Table 1 . Our PL quenching data demonstrate that PCzSi is among the best performing materials for this purpose.

The nitroaromatic explosives possess similar chemical and electronic structures; it is, therefore, hard to realize a selective detection by PL sensing. In order to test the selectivity of PCzSi sensor, PL sensing experiments towards other nitroaromatic compounds, TNT, TNB and 2,4-dinitrophenol (DNP) were also studied. As shown in Fig. 4 , PL quenching phenomena were observed towards all of the nitroaromatic compounds, but the quenching efficiencies were very different from that towards TNP. It took 1.5 mM of TNT and 1.7 mM of TNB, respectively, for 90% and 85% of PL quenching of PCzSi. From the Stern-Volmer plots (Fig. 4), the KSV constants towards TNT and TNB were calculated to be 2.0 × 103 M−1 and 1.5 × 103 M−1, respectively, which are much lower than that towards TNP. The LOD values were calculated to be 1.7 ppm and 1.9 ppm for TNT and TNB, respectively (Fig. S5 and S6, Table S2 and S3). The two orders of magnitude lower KSV and much higher LOD values towards TNT and TNB demonstrate a highly selective detection of TNP molecule based on PCzSi sensor. Another nitrophenol molecule, DNP was also tested here for comparison (Fig. 4). The successive decrease of PL intensity of PCzSi was observed as well upon adding DNP, with a lower quenching efficiency than that towards TNP, but higher than TNT and TNB. The KSV constant and LOD value were calculated to be 2.3 × 104 M−1 and 120 ppb (Fig. S7, Table S4), respectively. Therefore, the selective detection towards TNP over TNT and TNB is probably caused by their different photophysical properties. The sensing mechanism will be discussed to explain this result in the following paragraph. The selectivity property could be observed obviously in Fig. 5 , which was carried out by individually adding 20 μM of nitroaromatic molecules including DNP, TNT, TNB, DNT, nitrobenzene (NB), 4-nitrobenzoic acid (NBA), and phenol as comparison, followed by the addition of 20 μM of TNP. It can be seen that other competing analytes only caused little PL quenching effect, while the PL was rapidly and dramatically quenched upon the subsequent addition of TNP, thus demonstrating the little effect of them on the detection of TNP.

The ubiquitous PL sensing mechanism towards nitroaromatic analytes is known as photo-induced electron transfer, driven by the LUMO-LUMO offset between the PL material and nitroaromatic analyte. Herein, the LUMO of PCzSi (−2.20 eV) is higher than that of the detected nitro-explosives [62], which offers the photo-induced electron transfer from PCzSi to explosives, thus resulting in PL quenching. More nitro groups could benefit the PL quenching because of the greater contribution to LUMO with their electron-withdrawing ability, thus the sensitivity of PCzSi towards TNP is higher than DNP. The UV–vis absorption spectra of abovementioned nitro-explosives are very different, shown in Fig. 6 , which may offer other sensing mechanism. There is a great extent of spectral overlap between the UV–vis absorption spectra of DNP and TNP and both of the excitation and PL spectra of PCzSi, especially for TNP (Fig. 6), which demonstrates a high probability of resonance energy transfer mechanism. Therefore, with the combination of photo-induced electron transfer and resonance energy transfer mechanisms, PCzSi sensor exhibits a highly selective detection towards TNP over other nitro-explosives, such as TNT and TNB.

To explore the on-site practical application of our polymer sensor for nitroaromatic explosives detection, paper strips of PCzSi were fabricated for conducting the sensing experiments. As shown in Fig. 7 , TNP solutions with different concentrations were dipped onto the polymer paper, and a progressive PL quenching could be observed with increasing TNP amounts. It is worthy to note that the obvious PL quenching could be recognized with only 0.01 μg/mL of TNP solution. Besides, the TNP vapor could also cause a PL quenching of the polymer paper. Therefore, the result demonstrates a practical application of our polymer as PL solid sensor towards TNP.

A novel deep-blue polymer PCzSi based on carbazole and tetraphenylsilane was successfully synthesized, with high glass transition temperature and good thermal stability. When using for nitroaromatics sensing, PCzSi exhibited a highly sensitive detection of TNP, with a KSV constant up to 1.4 × 105 M−1 and a LOD of 38 ppb. Remarkably, a selective detection of TNP over TNT and TNB was realized using our polymer sensor, which shows a significant difference on KSV values of two orders of magnitude. The sensing mechanism towards TNP combining the photo-induced electron transfer and resonance energy transfer is responsible for the high selectivity. Furthermore, the paper strips test towards TNP solution and vapor demonstrates a good potential of our polymer for practical and on-site application as solid PL sensor for TNP detection.

Xue Guo: Investigation, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Bo Gao: Writing - review & editing. Xu Cui: carried out the experiments. Jihua Wang: carried out the experiments. Wenyue Dong: Funding acquisition, Project administration, Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing. Qian Duan: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Teng Fei: carried out the experiments. Zhongmin Su: Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank the financial support from Science and Technology Development Plan Project of Jilin Provinc (grant number 20200201235JC); the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant number 51803013); Jilin Association for Science and Technology (grant number 192011).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Luminescent triphenylamine-based metal–organic frameworks: recent advances in nitroaromatics detection
Dalton Trans

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Shi Z-Q, Ji N-N, Hu H-L. Luminescent triphenylamine-based metal-organic frameworks: recent advances in nitroaromatics detection. Dalton Trans 2020;49:12929-12939.

Polym Chem

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhou H, Chua MH, Tang BZ, Xu J. Aggregation-induced emission (AIE)-active polymers for explosive detection. Polym Chem 2019;10:3822-3840.

Mass Spectrom Rev

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Makinen M, Nousiainen M, Sillanpaa M. Ion spectrometric detection technologies for ultra-traces of explosives: a review. Mass Spectrom Rev 2011;30:940-973.

Talanta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Walsh ME. Determination of nitroaromatic, nitramine, and nitrate ester explosives in soil by gas chromatography and an electron capture detector. Talanta 2001;54:427-438.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simultaneous determination of carbazole-based explosives in environmental waters by dispersive liquid–liquid microextraction coupled to HPLC with UV–vis detection
Microchim Acta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Rahimi-Nasrabadi M, Zahedi MM, Pourmortazav SM, Heydari R, Rai H, Jazayeri J, Javidan A.
